answer:
Both Allama Iqbal's "Ek Pahar aur Gilehri" (*Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 21) and the anonymous Old English "Maxims I" (*Old English Elegies*, ch. 6) derive general ethical or cosmological maxims from concrete particulars, employing an inductive logic akin to fable (fabular): observable entities in nature or society exemplify universal principles of divine or natural order.[^1][^2] However, their logics diverge in structure, tone, and telos—Iqbal's is dynamic and dialogic, resolving conflict through witty reversal; "Maxims I" is static and declarative, accumulating static truths in a gnomic catalog.

### Shared Fabular Logic: Particulars as Exemplars of Order
Both texts treat particulars not as isolated anecdotes but as microcosms revealing macro-order. Iqbal pits a haughty mountain (immense, immobile) against a nimble squirrel (tiny, agile), generalizing to God's purposeful creation: "In everything, the might of God is manifest; / His wisdom makes some large, and small He makes the rest."[^3] The mountain's bulk enables no "virtue but your bigness," while the squirrel cracks nuts—each fulfills its niche, yielding the capstone: "No single thing in this world is in vain; / In Nature's great workshop, no creature is plain."[^4] This mirrors "Maxims I"'s parade of particulars (frost, fire, trees, kings, wives, wolves), each "must" obeying necessity: "Frost must freeze, fire melt wood, / earth must grow, ice must form a bridge," or "A warrior must be on his horse's back."[^5] Both invoke the divine artisan—God as measurer in "Maxims I" ("the Measurer raised up... lands"), Iqbal's "workshop"—to affirm hierarchy-with-purpose, where size, role, or season implies teleology.[^6]

### Divergences: Narrative Reversal vs. Gnomic Assertion
Iqbal's logic is *fabulist* proper: anthropomorphic debate enacts hubris (mountain's size-boast) and comeuppance (squirrel's functional retort), climaxing in synthetic moral. The particular conflict (big vs. small) negates raw magnitude for relational utility, theologically democratizing value ("you are not as small as I am").[^7] This echoes Aesopic fables, with the weak upending the strong via praxis (nut-cracking challenge).

"Maxims I," by contrast, is *gnomic*: non-narrative, paratactic list without plot or victor. Particulars assert self-contained "musts" ("A wife should keep faith... though often she is blamed for vices"; "The friendless... takes wolves for his companions"), evoking widsith-like catalog (world surveyed, truths tabulated).[^8] No reversal—frost *simply* freezes; wolves *simply* betray—cumulatively sketching cosmic/social *gesceaft* (order). The opening frames this as communal exchange ("Wise men should exchange sayings"), but delivery is monologic, impersonal.[^9]

Iqbal's fable thus *resolves* tension (pride humbled, unity affirmed); "Maxims I" *describes* it (fate inexorable, roles fixed). Where Iqbal proves maxim via squirrel's agency, "Maxims I" states it via observation, closer to proverb than plot.

[^1]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 21, [translation](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/21-ek-pahar-aur-gilehri/translation/).
[^2]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 6, [translation](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/06-maxims-i/translation/).
[^3]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 21, [p-fa7390](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/21-ek-pahar-aur-gilehri/translation/#p-fa7390).
[^4]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 21, [p-4ddc6a](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/21-ek-pahar-aur-gilehri/translation/#p-4ddc6a).
[^5]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 6, [p-84cf09](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/06-maxims-i/translation/#p-84cf09).
[^6]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 6, get_passage on range covering "Spacious lands the Measurer..."; cf. Iqbal [p-c92fa2](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/21-ek-pahar-aur-gilehri/translation/#p-c92fa2).
[^7]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 21, [p-4fdf79](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/21-ek-pahar-aur-gilehri/translation/#p-4fdf79).
[^8]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 6, [p-84cf09](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/06-maxims-i/translation/#p-84cf09).
[^9]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 6, opening [p-84cf09](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/06-maxims-i/translation/#p-84cf09).
